turns.content: I'm following a talk on iterators and I wrote this code to build a CSV row by collecting chars into a String, but it feels clunky. Is there an idiomatic way to collect from an iterator into a String instead of manually pushing each character?

You're close; the idiom is using collect::<String>() on your iterator, which consumes the iterator and builds a String in one step. However, if you already have a Vec<char>, calling collect::<String>() won't work because that type doesn't implement Into<String>. You likely need to map each char to a string slice or use join with a separator instead of collecting raw characters into a single String unless your goal is just concatenation without separators.

Ah, I see. But if I have an iterator yielding individual strings like &str for columns, how do I collect them into one CSV row separated by commas? My current approach pushes to Vec and then iterates again which feels inefficient. Is there a way to avoid that double iteration?

The idiomatic pattern is using itertools::Itertools or standard iterator chaining with flat_map followed by join, but for simple column collection into a CSV row you typically map each value to its string representation and then use String::from_iter with the separator. To make this truly efficient without double iteration, consider using fold or collect after mapping to avoid intermediate storage.

Wait, I think I misunderstood. If I have an iterator of &str values representing columns, can I just map them to a Vec<String> and then use join? Or is there something more direct with iterators that doesn't require the intermediate Vec step?

You're very close; you don't need an intermediate Vec if you chain operations. The idiomatic Rust way is to map each column value to its string form, then use String::from_iter or join on a collection of strings. But I have a clarifying question: are your values already in &str format from a file parser, or do they come as some other type like Option<T> that needs handling before joining?

They come as Option<&str>, so some columns might be missing. Can I collect those into a String with commas while skipping None values without writing extra code for filtering? Is there an iterator idiom I'm missing here?

Yes, you can use the filter method on iterators to skip None values before collecting them into a String using join or from_iter. The idiomatic pattern is to map each Option<&str> to &str by flattening with flat_map or using collect::<Vec<_>> then joining, but since you want to avoid intermediate Vecs, prefer chaining methods like filter_then_collect_to_string. This avoids storing the full list in memory and lets the String grow directly from the filtered iterator stream.

I'm trying to build a CSV row by iterating over chars and collecting them, but I can't seem to get the string right. Why does collect::<String>() even work on an iterator of characters when I haven't explicitly implemented anything?

That's a great question about how Rust iterators actually behave. The key is that String implements DerefMut and has a trait for collecting from iterables, so you don't need to manually call any function on the string itself. You're using the collect method which takes an iterator and builds a String by calling push or insert methods internally as it consumes each char from your source.
So I just pass my char iterator directly into collect without any extra steps? I thought I had to write out the loop manually.

Exactly, that's the beauty of Rust idioms here. You don't need a manual loop because collect handles the iteration and string building for you automatically. This lets you chain operations like map or filter before collecting into your final String without breaking the flow.
